odds.panelTitle: This probability doesn’t really register, does it?
odds.panelDesc: 1 in 292 million doesn’t feel real. So we compared it to things that do.
odds.callout: 💡 Winning the Powerball jackpot is about 19,000 times less likely than getting struck by lightning in your lifetime. Yet several people still win every year 🍀
odds.winnersTitle: 🏆 People really have won this much
odds.winnersDesc: It does actually happen — here are some record-breaking real cases
odds.historyDesc: Jackpot amounts we’ve checked, with estimated take-home by country (including the top 5 all-time records)
odds.historyFullLink: 📄 See the Top 5 all-time jackpots in detail →
odds.winner1: November 2022, California — Edwin Castro, winner of the largest Powerball jackpot in history. He took the $997.6M lump sum and bought $25.5M and $47M houses back to back. Sued over a claim the ticket was stolen, he was confirmed the rightful owner in 2024, then in 2025 donated land to build homes for wildfire victims.
odds.winner2: August 2017, Massachusetts — Mavis Wanczyk quit her job at a hospital where she’d worked for 32 years the moment she found out she’d won. Once she became famous, though, dozens of fake accounts impersonating her popped up online, and police had to step in.
odds.winner3: Some states let winners stay anonymous (Florida, Texas, Delaware, etc.). If you’d rather not end up like Castro or Wanczyk, this is the option many big winners choose.
odds.numbersTitle: 🔢 Curious about the most frequently drawn numbers ever?
odds.numbersDesc: Real draw records since the format changed in 2015
odds.numbersPbText: Main numbers 21, 61, 64, 28, 23 come up often, and for the Powerball number itself, sites most often mention 4, 18, 21, 24
odds.numbersMmText: Main numbers 31, 17, 10, 48 come up often, and 22 is the most frequently drawn Mega Ball
odds.numbersCallout: Every draw is completely independent, a number coming up often before does not make it more likely next time. Just for fun!
odds.lifeTitle: 💫 How does life really change before and after winning?
odds.lifeDesc: Not rumors, things confirmed by actual research
odds.life1Tag: Most winners go broke within a few years?
odds.life1: This is an unfounded myth. The National Endowment for Financial Education, which supposedly published the 70% go broke stat, officially denied ever conducting such a study. A 15-year Swedish tracking study found winners actually kept their wealth better than non-winners.
odds.life2: Contrary to rumors, the change is nowhere near that dramatic — divorce rates only rise modestly. What is more interesting: friendships get shaken up more than marriages, since money often triggers a reshuffling of who your real friends are.
odds.life3Tag: Winners quit their job right away?
odds.life3: It depends on the amount, winners of over 10 million dollars mostly do leave the workforce, but below that, about half keep working. It seems the connection to other people and sense of purpose matters more than the money itself.
odds.life4Tag: So, does it make you happier?
odds.life4: Day-to-day mood does not jump dramatically, but the feeling of being satisfied with my life stays noticeably higher for over 10 years. The real challenge is not managing the money, it is managing family and friends expectations.
odds.lightningDesc: Drawn just for fun, with today’s "lightning" energy — 6 random numbers
odds.drawNote: Your odds with these numbers are still 1 in 292 million — just for fun 😉
odds.payoutTitle: How much do you win for each match?
odds.payoutDesc: Powerball prize tiers by numbers matched
odds.megaNote: 💡 Mega Millions has different numbers and prizes — check the official site →
odds.howtoText: Powerball is a game of matching 5 regular numbers (from 1–69) plus 1 Powerball number (from 1–26) — 6 numbers total. The prize depends on how many regular numbers you match, plus whether you match the Powerball.
odds.jcStep3: What actually lands in your hand after tax
odds.jcStep4: 💡 What if you take the annuity instead of the lump sum?
odds.jcAnnuityYearNet: What you actually get each year
odds.jcAnnuityNote: Unlike the discounted lump sum, an annuity pays out the full announced jackpot over 30 payments across 29 years (each payment grows 5% after the first). Spreading it out over years also spreads the tax burden — the amount above is a simple average, so the actual first payment is lower.
odds.disclaimer: Odds figures are based on official published data. KRW conversion uses a reference exchange rate and may differ from actual amounts.
faq.basisNote: 💡 Showing only the questions relevant to the tax basis (Korea/US/China) you chose on the home screen
faq.q1: How much tax is actually deducted? 😮
faq.a1: US residents get 24% withheld upfront, settled up to 37% later. Non-US residents (including Korean and Chinese residents) generally face a 30% withholding rule.
faq.q2: Wait, I pay tax in Korea too?
faq.a2: Yes. US lottery winnings aren’t a domestic lottery, so they’re combined into comprehensive income tax (up to 45%). Tax already paid in the US can offset via Foreign Tax Credit (FTC).
faq.q3: Can I get any of the tax back? 💸
faq.a3: The 30% US federal withholding is practically non-refundable. State tax varies — Maryland and Arizona are unusual in taxing non-residents too. Pick your winning state below to check.
faq.selectState: Select the state you won in
faq.selectStatePlaceholder: Select the state you won in 👇
faq.wizardDefault: Select a state to see your refund possibility instantly
faq.q4: Will my health insurance premium spike if I win? 🏥
faq.a4: Regional subscribers see winnings fully counted as income, raising premiums a lot. Employed subscribers pay extra premium above ₩20M/year in outside income too. If registered as a dependent, you may lose that status.
faq.q5: I’m already receiving National Pension / Basic Pension — does this affect it? 👴
faq.a5: Depends on the program. National Pension — if already receiving it, the reduction rule applies to earned/business income, not a one-time windfall. Basic Pension / welfare benefits reassess eligibility by income and assets, so benefits could change. (Basic Pension is generally for Korean nationals only — foreign residents in Korea only need to check the National Pension reduction rule.)
faq.q6: How do I tell if a "you won" message is a scam? 🚨
faq.a6: Common scam patterns: impersonating card companies asking for card info to "refund" a losing ticket, fake lottery commission asking for crypto purchases, or selling fake winning-number predictions. Real winnings are checked by you on the official site — unsolicited requests for money or info are 100% scams.
faq.q7: How much gift tax if I share with family? 👨‍👩‍👧
faq.a7: Spouse up to ₩600M, adult children up to ₩50M are tax-free. Amounts above that face a 10–50% progressive rate. Filing on time gets you a 3% credit.
faq.q7us: How much gift tax if I share with family? 👨‍👩‍👧
faq.a7us: As of 2026, you can give up to $19,000 per person per year with no filing required at all. Give more than that, and you’ll need to file a gift tax return — but you still won’t actually owe any tax until your lifetime gifts pass the $15 million exemption. For almost everyone, that means you can share freely without worrying about gift tax.
faq.q7cn: How much gift tax if I share with family? 👨‍👩‍👧
faq.a7cn: China has no dedicated gift tax on cash gifts between individuals — you don’t need to worry about tax when giving cash to a spouse, parent, or child. Gifting real estate is a separate matter with its own taxes (like deed tax), so check that separately if it applies.
faq.q7in: How much gift tax if I share with family? 👨‍👩‍👧
faq.a7in: Gifts to specified relatives (spouse, parents, siblings, children, etc.) are tax-free regardless of amount. However, cash gifts or receipts exceeding ₹200,000 (about 2 lakh) can trigger a separate penalty under Section 269ST of the Income Tax Act, so it's safer to split large amounts via bank transfer.
faq.q8: Is prize money subject to property division in a divorce? 💍
faq.a8: In principle, no — courts treat it as personal property from pure luck. But if a spouse helped preserve or grow the money afterward, that contribution may be included.
faq.q9: By when do I have to claim the winnings? ⏰
faq.a9: Usually 90 days to 1 year. Miss it, and it reverts to the state — gone forever. One winner actually lost $138M by missing the deadline.
faq.q10: Aside from the lottery, might I have unclaimed money that’s already mine?
faq.a10: Unlike the lottery, this is money already confirmed under your name — old tax refunds, forgotten bank accounts, unclaimed retirement plans, and more. Billions of dollars in unclaimed property sit unclaimed in the US every year. Check the list below.
faq.checklistTitle: Check if any of these apply to you
faq.check1: Never claimed a state or federal tax refund you were actually owed
faq.check2: Worked as a freelancer/contractor and might have skipped filing some year
faq.check3: Left a job mid-year and never checked if you overpaid that year’s taxes
faq.check4: Might have missed deductions (medical, donations, etc.) in some year
faq.check5: Not sure where to even start — want a general starting point
faq.check6: Have an old bank account, uncashed check, or insurance payout you forgot about
faq.check7: Changed jobs and never rolled over an old 401(k) or pension plan
faq.check8: Own U.S. Savings Bonds that matured years ago and stopped earning interest
faq.checkDefault: If any apply, check for real below 👇
faq.checklistFooter: We can’t do the lookup for you — a couple of these (MissingMoney, USA.gov) just need your name, but others (IRS, retirement plans, savings bonds) will ask for your SSN, the same way Korean sites ask for your resident registration number. Either way, these are the real official channels. Takes about 10 minutes to check.
faq.q11: Is it better to take it all at once, or in installments?
faq.a11: US lottery jackpots can be paid two ways — ① annuity (spread over 29 years) or ② lump sum (paid at once, but only about 45–60% of the announced amount). The jackpot in the news is based on ①. Most winners choose ② — check the for a detailed comparison.
faq.q12: Where can I buy a US lottery ticket?
faq.a12: Sold at convenience stores and gas stations in the US. Domestic purchase-agent services carry legal risk, so we don’t refer them.
faq.q13: What if a friend buys a ticket for me, or claims the prize on my behalf?
faq.a13: Even a friend buying it personally creates complications. Ownership is usually determined by who signs to claim it. If they claim as a US resident, their tax rate applies, and transferring the money could trigger US gift tax on their end, plus gift tax rules in whichever country you live in.
faq.q14: Where can I check past winning numbers?
faq.a14b: We show recent numbers on our home screen, but the official sites are the most accurate source.
faq.q15: Can I claim winnings without US citizenship?
faq.a15: Yes, foreigners who legally bought a ticket can claim. Non-resident aliens face the 30% withholding though.
faq.q16: I’m a Korean living in the US — which option should I choose? (students, expats, green card, citizens)
faq.a16: “Living in the US” and “US tax resident” can differ. Green card/citizenship holders should pick “US.” Students or early-stage expats may still be non-resident aliens, making “Korea (non-resident)” more accurate. Consult a US tax pro if unsure.
faq.q17: What is the "filing status (single/married)" option in the calculator?
faq.a17: US filing status affects tax brackets, but for lottery-size amounts it barely matters — the difference between single and joint top brackets is a rounding error.
faq.q18: What actually happens, step by step, if I win?
faq.a18: It’s a 6-step process: buy in the US → confirm the win → claim it. See the full breakdown under A closer look at the purchase process below.
faq.q19: How do I file these taxes?
faq.a19: The US withholds 30% first; in Korea you report it as other income the following May. Tax paid in the US can offset via FTC. Consult a tax pro handling both countries.
faq.q19us: How do I file these taxes?
faq.a19us: As a US resident, you report the winnings as "Other Income" on your federal return (Form 1040, Schedule 1). For large amounts you’ll typically get a W-2G showing 24% withheld upfront — that’s just a down payment against your actual tax rate (up to 37%), so you may owe more or get some back when you file. Depending on the state, you may also need to file a separate state return.
faq.q19cn: How do I file these taxes?
faq.a19cn: The US withholds 30% first. In China, this income is classified as "incidental income" (偶然所得) taxed at a flat 20%, which you self-report between March 1 and June 30 of the following year via the "个人所得税" app or the natural person e-tax portal (per MOF/STA Announcement No. 3 of 2020). Tax already paid in the US can offset this through the foreign tax credit.
faq.q19in: How do I actually file the tax return?
faq.a19in: The US withholds 30% first. In India, you report this prize income under Schedule FSI (Foreign Source Income) of your income tax return (ITR). To claim a foreign tax credit for the US withholding, you must also file Form 67 (reorganized as Form 44 under the 2026 rules) by the ITR filing deadline, and the amount in it must exactly match Schedule FSI.
faq.q20: How do I claim the Foreign Tax Credit (FTC)? What documents do I need?
faq.a20: To offset the US tax (30% withholding) against your Korean income tax filing, based on a July 2026 National Tax Service phone consultation you’ll need: a certificate of tax payment issued by the foreign (US) government, a payment receipt, a withholding tax receipt, and proof of the prize itself. The exchange rate used is the rate on the day you actually received the prize money (per Enforcement Decree of the Income Tax Act, Article 50). This isn’t an official written ruling, so confirm the exact document list with a tax professional before filing.
faq.q21: Do I owe Korean tax even if I haven't lived in Korea long? ⚠️
faq.a21: This isn't a simple yes/no. Under a proviso in Article 3 of Korea's Income Tax Act, a resident who has lived in Korea 5 years or less within the most recent 10 years (based on length of residence, not nationality) has a special rule: foreign-source income is only taxed in Korea if it's actually remitted to or received within Korea. However, whether this actually applies to migrant workers (e.g. E-9 visa holders) is unclear and may be interpreted differently case by case — if you already have your life based in Korea and are working continuously, tax authorities may view it differently. Don't assume "no remittance means no tax" — if you really do win, talk to a tax professional right away to get an answer specific to your situation.
faq.moreTitle: 📚 Learn more (terms, purchase process, payout methods, etc.)
faq.termsTitle: Let’s start with the basic terms
faq.termsDesc: The part people mix up the most — here’s a side-by-side comparison of the two games
faq.samePlace: 📍 Where to buy: Same place! Both are sold at the same convenience stores and gas stations
faq.sameStoreCallout: 💡 Many wonder "where do I buy Powerball vs Mega Millions?" — they’re actually sold at the same stores. Just tell the cashier which game you want.
faq.otherGamesNote: Note: there’s also Millionaire for Life, Lotto America, and state-specific lotteries, but only these two make Korean news, so that’s our focus.
faq.taxGuideTitle: The tax basics, in a nutshell
faq.tg3Title: Lump sum vs. annuity — what’s the difference?
faq.tg3Sub: Different tax brackets apply, so total tax owed can differ
faq.purchaseGuideTitle: A closer look at the purchase process
faq.gateText:
You need to actually be in the US
(travel, business trip, studying abroad, etc.)
faq.gateSub: There’s no legal way to buy remotely from Korea
faq.step1Detail: Any convenience store, gas station, or supermarket with a ‘Lottery’ sign works
faq.ticketMeta: Draw date: MON JUL 06 2026  |  QP (Quick Pick)  |  $2.00
faq.ticketDetail: The front shows your chosen numbers (or Quick Pick random numbers), the draw date, price, and a scannable barcode. That barcode is essential for verifying a win later — keep it safe!
faq.step2FlowDetail: Usually $2–$5 per ticket. You pay right at the counter.
faq.phraseTitle: 🗣️ Useful phrases even if you don’t speak English
faq.phraseTip: 💡 Just say "quick pick" and the computer picks random numbers — most people buy this way!
faq.playslipTitle: ✏️ Want to pick your own numbers? (How to fill out a play slip)
faq.psStep1: Grab a free play slip from the store, and fill in bubbles with a black or blue pen (or pencil) — fill the circles solidly (red pen won’t scan)
faq.psStep2: You can pick some numbers yourself and mark QP (Quick Pick) for the rest — only those get filled randomly
faq.psStep3: If you make a mistake, don’t erase — mark the VOID box and fill in the next row
faq.psStep4: Hand the completed slip to the cashier, who scans it on the spot to turn it into a real ticket (the slip itself isn’t a ticket!)
faq.signBackNote: 💡 If you win, sign the back of the ticket immediately — this is an important step to prove ownership if lost
faq.step3Detail: Most states require you to be 18 or older (some require 21). You can buy regardless of nationality.
faq.step4Title: 📢 Want to check past winning numbers?
faq.step4Detail: You can check winning numbers directly on the official sites.
faq.checkWinTitle: 💡 Here’s how to check if your ticket won
faq.checkMethod1: Compare the numbers yourself — the most reliable method
faq.checkMethod2: Scan the barcode with the state lottery’s official app
faq.checkMethod3: Use the store’s self-service scanner or ask staff
faq.step5Detail: Visit the lottery office for that state in person with your ID and ticket. The deadline is usually 180 days to 1 year
faq.step6Title: Lump sum or annuity? You must choose when claiming
faq.step6Detail: After claiming, you usually have 60 days to choose one or the other. If you don’t choose in time, it defaults to the annuity.
faq.lumpSumDetail: Receive about 45–60% of the announced amount all at once. The option most winners choose
faq.annuityDetail: Full announced amount paid over 29 years in 30 installments (first immediate, +5%/year after)
faq.payoutIrreversible: This choice is irreversible — decide only after consulting a tax/financial professional
faq.sixStepsNote: That’s all 6 steps. The key point is that if step 1 (the prerequisite) isn’t met, none of the later steps apply to you.
faq.payMethodTitle: Do you get paid in cash or by bank transfer?
faq.payMethodDesc: Completely different depending on the amount
faq.small600Detail: You can get cash right on the spot at the store where you bought the ticket.
faq.largeAmt: 🏦 Large amounts (hundreds of thousands to millions of dollars)
faq.largeAmtDetail: Not paid in cash. Paid by check or bank transfer, usually taking 6–8 weeks.
faq.krResidentDetail: There’s no clearly published official process. If this happens, proceed together with the lottery office, a bank capable of international transfer, and a tax/legal professional.
faq.howManyTitle: How many tickets can I buy at once?
faq.howManyDesc: Actually two different questions mixed together
faq.multiTicketQ: Q. Can I buy multiple tickets at once?
faq.multiTicketA: Yes, no legal limit. A play slip usually allows up to 5 games per slip.
faq.multiDrawQ: Q. Can I enter the same numbers into multiple future drawings at once?
faq.multiDrawA: Yes, via ‘multi-draw’ — usually 10–26 draws at once depending on the state.
faq.noRefundNote: Note: tickets can’t be refunded or canceled once purchased (all sales are final)
faq.finalDisclaimer: If you have any other questions, feel free to reach out via Contact!
privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.b1:
ChamTax (the "Site") does not store calculator input values (prize amount, country of residence, exchange rate, etc.) on any server. All calculations happen only within the visitor’s browser and disappear when you leave the page.
However, if you contact us via "Contact," we collect only the email address and message content you provide.
privacy.b2: Used only to review and respond to inquiries — never for marketing or other purposes.
privacy.b3: Retained for up to 1 year after the inquiry is resolved, then promptly destroyed.
privacy.b4: We do not share collected personal information with third parties. However, ads displayed on the site (Google AdSense) and visit analytics (Google Analytics) may independently collect visit information via cookies through Google, which is handled under Google’s privacy policy, not this site’s.
privacy.b5: Cookies from third-party ad services such as Google may be used to serve ads, and Google Analytics cookies are used to analyze visitor statistics. You can refuse or delete cookies in your browser settings, though this may limit ad personalization and analytics features.
privacy.b6: You may request to view, correct, or delete any personal information you provided via inquiries at any time. Please contact us via the Contact page below.
disclaimer.title: Disclaimer
disclaimer.b1: All calculation results and information provided on this site are a reference simulation, not tax, legal, or financial advice.
disclaimer.b2: The tax rates, exchange rates, etc. used in the calculator are simplified examples based on tax law and may differ from actual tax owed. Regarding how foreign lottery winnings are taxed under Korean law, the calculator reflects an answer received via a July 2026 NTS online consultation ("comprehensive income tax combined, Foreign Tax Credit applicable"), but this is a consultation response, not an official binding ruling, and actual application may vary by circumstance (see FAQ for details).
disclaimer.b3: If you actually win a lottery and need to make decisions such as tax filing, please be sure to consult a professional such as a tax accountant who handles both Korean and US tax matters. This site is not liable for decisions made based solely on information provided here.
disclaimer.b4: This site does not act as an agent or broker for lottery ticket purchases and does not provide any purchase channels. Acting as a purchase agent for foreign lotteries within Korea may be subject to penalties under relevant law (e.g., Criminal Act Article 248).
disclaimer.b5: Powerball®, Mega Millions®, etc. are registered trademarks of their respective operating organizations (MUSL, etc.). This site is an independent information service with no affiliation or partnership with those organizations.
disclaimer.b6: To the extent permitted by law, the operator of this site is not liable for direct or indirect damages arising from use of the site, reliance on calculation results, or errors in information.
disclaimer.b7: The content, tax rate information, and policies of this site may change without prior notice.
contact.title: Let us know if something’s off or you have questions
contact.desc: If a calculation seems wrong or you’re curious about something, feel free to leave a message :)
contact.success: ✓ Your message was sent, thank you!
contact.error: Something went wrong sending this — please try again shortly.
footer.copy: This site is an informational simulator and does not act as an agent or broker for lottery purchases.
home.miniResultLabel: ✏️ Want to start from your desired take-home amount?
home.flowExplain1: 💡 US lottery jackpots can be paid two ways — ① Annuity: the full amount spread over 29 years, or ② Lump sum: paid all at once, but reduced to about 45–60% of that. The jackpot amount in the news is based on ①. This calculator uses ② the lump sum, pre-tax amount, since that's what most winners choose.
home.annuityInfoLink: 📅 So how different is choosing the annuity (①)? →
home.groundingNote: 🌱 But honestly, what you already have might be more than enough for a good day
home.dreamIntro: What would you do first if you won?
home.dreamFreedom: 🕊️ Hand in my resignation letter first
home.dreamFamily: 🏠 Buy a house for every family member
home.dreamTravel: ✈️ Leave on a world trip right away
home.dreamCalm: 💼 Just quietly check my bank balance
